setting.note: In the provincial Galician town of his childhood, the narrator conjures a dreamlike, autobiographical world around his eccentric father, a textile merchant whose shop and mind decay into ever more fantastical metamorphoses. Schulz roots his hallucinatory prose in the real Drohobycz: the cinnamon-scented family house, the father's cloth shop on the market square, the town's ancient heart, and the shabby, garish new commercial district he calls the Street of Crocodiles.

rights.originalWork: Public domain. Bruno Schulz (d. 1942), first published 1934 (Sklepy cynamonowe). Mapped as a cited work: we store no text and reproduce no passages here. The places are facts and the notes are our own.
rights.textSource: None stored. This entry reproduces no text; the public-domain original is widely available.
rights.territoryNotes: Locations and original commentary only. The original text is in the public domain.
rights.attribution: The Street of Crocodiles, Bruno Schulz (1934). Locations mapped by Read the Places.

palette.note: The cinnamon-brown of the shops that give the original its title, the warm, spiced dusk of the father's house before it slides into autumnal decay. A cited book's colour is not a quotation, because we store no text to quote; it still has to be legible, and this clears WCAG AA on paper.
